The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179

class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): grouper: t.Any list: t.List # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print # out the value during debugging. def __repr__(self) -> str: return tuple.__repr__(self) def __str__(self) -> str: return tuple.__str__(self) class _GroupTupleWithCount(t.NamedTuple): grouper: t.Any list: t.List count: int # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print # out the value during debugging. def __repr__(self) -> str: return tuple.__repr__(self) def __str__(self) -> str: return tuple.__str__(self) @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.Union[t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]], t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V], int]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) if with_counts: result_with_counts = [] for key, values in groups: values_list = list(values) result_with_counts.append( _GroupTupleWithCount(key, values_list, len(values_list)) ) return result_with_counts return [_GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groups] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.Union[t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]], t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V], int]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) if with_counts: result_with_counts = [] for key, values in groups: values_list = await auto_to_list(values) result_with_counts.append( _GroupTupleWithCount(key, values_list, len(values_list)) ) return result_with_counts return [_GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groups]

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}

    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}

``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja

You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja

You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: jinja

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
t = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }};{% endfor %}')
print(t.render(users=[{'city':'NY'},{'city':'NY'},{'city':'LA'}]))
t2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }};{% endfor %}')
print(t2.render(users=[{'city':'NY'},{'city':'NY'},{'city':'LA'}]))
"

content.text: Both work correctly. Now let's run the existing test suite for filters to make sure nothing broke.
Let's also run the async filter test variant and the full filter suite quickly.

content.text: The patch is written to `patch.txt`. Summary: added an optional `with_counts` parameter to the `groupby` filter (both sync and async variants) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`. When `True`, it yields `(grouper, list, count)` tuples via a new `_GroupTupleWithCount` namedtuple instead of the default `(grouper, list)`; defaults to `False` for backward compatibility. Verified with a manual render test and the existing `test_filters.py`/`test_async_filters.py` suites (163 passed).
